Market Review

Risk assets struggled given uncertainty as President Trump’s administration spurred uncertainty and risk market volatility relating to its tariff policies.

The administration implemented additional 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico (with Canadian energy an exception at 10%). However, policymakers quickly put the tariffs on a 30-day pause. After the pause, the administration implemented the tariffs but subsequently amended them to exempt imports satisfying United States–Mexico–Canada Agreement (USMCA) rules of origin requirements. The administration also enforced additional 10% tariffs on China and 25% blanket tariffs on steel and aluminum imports from most countries (amending tariffs implemented in 2018). This prompted retaliations from Canada, China and the European Union (EU). The administration also announced 25% tariffs on autos and 25% tariffs on those buying oil from Venezuela, to take effect in April.

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) announced a slowdown in “quantitative tightening” (QT). From April it will allow a limit of $5bn of Treasuries per month to “roll off” its balance sheet, down from $25bn. It left its cap on agency Mortgage-backed securities (MBS) unchanged at $35bn. The central bank’s quarterly summary of economic projections reflected higher inflation and lower growth forecasts for 2025, with tariffs a major factor. It forecast headline and core inflation at 2.7% and 2.8%, respectively at the end of this year, both up from its 2.5% forecast in December. It also projected Gross Domestic Product (GDP) at 1.7% this year, down from its previous 2.1% forecast. However, the committee made no changes to its median “dot plot” Fed funds rate projections.

Inflation was relatively stable over the period. Headline Consumer price index (CPI) rose slightly from 2.7% to 2.8% and core CPI fell from 3.3% to 3.1%. PCE remained at 2.5% while Core Personal consumption expenditures (PCE) remained at 2.8%. The data did, however, show some encouraging progress in stubborn core services sectors, indicating continued disinflationary momentum ahead of tariff impacts. Consumer confidence deteriorated, however, with the University of Michigan and Conference Board’s consumer survey reporting consumer 1-year and 5-year inflation expectations at 5% and 4.1% per annum (pa) respectively.

The labor market was also relatively stable, with the 6-month rolling average of monthly payroll gains trending at ~180,000. The household survey was weaker, however, with employment falling 588,000. The unemployment rate fell from 4.2% to 4.1%. Notably, however, the U6 underemployment rate (a broader measure that includes those that have stopped looking for work and involuntary part-time workers) rose from 7.7% to 8%, the highest since October 2021.

Equity markets fell sharply, with the S&P 500 and Magnificent 7 indices delivering 4.3% and -16% total returns respectively. Treasury yields fell by up to 43bp across the curve in a flight to quality move. The Bloomberg US Corporate Investment Grade index and the Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield index delivered excess returns of -0.85% and -1.13% respective. However, on a total return basis both indices still ended the quarter in positive territory, at 2.31% and 1% respective, driven by contributions from duration exposure and coupon income.

Performance Review

Underperformance was primarily due to the fund's overweight allocation to risk assets, specifically high yield and investment grade credit. The main positioning change involved adding modest yield and optimizing security selection.

Duration and yield curve positioning was a slight positive during the period. The fund entered the period with a curve steepening bias, which contributed negatively versus the benchmark as the US Treasury curve flattened, albeit we achieved alpha from the overall long duration bias within the fund. There were relative value trades within certain non-US rates which were a modest detractor from performance. However, we took profits on other rates trades, including our euro inflation overweight, short positions in Japanese government bonds, and short positions in Canada and Sweden versus Australia. We maintained the fund’s overweight position in US 5- year duration and UK 30-year duration.

From a sector perspective, the fund’s energy overweight underperformed, as did the auto, and utility overweights. Security selection had a minor negative impact, mainly owing to Agency MBS coupon positioning. We adjusted the fund’s agency MBS allocation to focus on higher coupon exposure, a more defensive posture with respect to interest rate volatility. During January and February, the fund increased its exposure to corporate credit by approximately 2%, focusing on high yield and US domestic tickers. This included a modest but selective increase in exposure to domestic energy and cyclical sectors, such as autos, while decreasing exposure to transportation, financial, and non-cyclical sectors.

Market Outlook

Politics will remain uncertain, but most economists believe tariffs will result in higher near-term inflation and cause a drag on growth. Overall, however, we remain cautiously positive on investment grade credit. Spread repricing, relatively high absolute yields and the contractual nature of may continue to draw investors towards credit markets given equity volatility and valuations.

We expect issuance to remain elevated through the first half of 2025 as corporate treasurers seek to take advantage of demand. This could create attractive opportunities to add exposure cheaply. A significant driver of returns might be the extent the headwind to growth from tariffs could be offset by interest rates. We expect politically induced volatility to offer significant opportunities for active managers, particularly those focused on security and sector selection as well as those able to take a global approach.

Additionally, on 10/19/2018 the Fund received the merged assets of Dreyfus Intermediate Term Income Fund, the performance of which is not reflected above.

Risks

Bonds are subject to interest-rate, credit, liquidity, call and market risks, to varying degrees. Generally, all other factors being equal, bond prices are inversely related to interest-rate changes and rate increases can cause price declines. The use of derivatives involves risks different from, or possibly greater than, the risks associated with investing directly in the underlying assets. Derivatives can be highly volatile, illiquid, and difficult to value and there is the risk that changes in the value of a derivative held by the portfolio will not correlate with the underlying instruments or the portfolio’s other investments. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging-market countries. High yield bonds involve increased credit and liquidity risk than higher-rated bonds and are considered speculative in terms of the issuer’s ability to pay interest and repay principal on a timely basis. Mortgage-backed securities: Ginnie Maes and other securities backed by the full faith and credit of the United States government are guaranteed only as to the timely payment of interest and principal when held to maturity. The market prices for such securities are not guaranteed and will fluctuate. Privately issued mortgage-related securities also are subject to credit risks associated with the underlying mortgage properties. These securities may be more volatile and less liquid than more traditional, government-backed debt securities.

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based flagship benchmark that measures the investment-grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. The index includes Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM pass-throughs), ABS and CMBS (agency and nonagency). The Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index measures the investment grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market. It includes USD-denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility and financial issuers. The Bloomberg High Yield Bond Index measures the USD-denominated, high yield, fixed-rate corporate bond market. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody’s, Fitch and S&P is Ba1/BB+/BB+ or below. Bonds from issuers with an emerging markets country of risk, based on Bloomberg’s EM country definition, are excluded. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

Mortgage-Backed Security (MBS) is an investment similar to a bond that is made up of a bundle of home loans bought from the banks that issued them. Investors in MBS receive periodic payments similar to bond coupon payments. Asset-Backed Security (ABS) is a financial security such as a bond or note which is collateralized by a pool of assets such as loans, leases, credit card debt, royalties, or receivables. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a measure that examines the weighted average of prices of a basket of consumer goods and services, such as transportation, food, and medical care. A yield curve is a line that plots yields, or interest rates, of bonds that have equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is the total monetary or market value of all the finished goods and services produced within a country’s borders in a specific time period. Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) also known as consumer spending, is a measure of the spending on goods and services by people of the United States. A yield curve is a line that plots the yields or interest rates of bonds that have equal credit quality but different maturity dates. Investment grade refers to the quality of a company's credit. To be considered an investment grade issue, the company must be rated at 'BBB' or higher by Standard and Poor's or 'Baa" or higher by Moody's. The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) is the Federal Reserve's policymaking body responsible for setting and implementing monetary policy in the United States, primarily through open market operations to influence interest rates and the money supply. Quantitative tightening (QT) is a contractionary monetary policy used by central banks to reduce liquidity and the money supply in the economy, typically after a period of quantitative easing (QE), by selling assets or allowing them to mature without reinvesting the proceeds.

data.title: AMD Is Poised For A Sharp Rebound To $175
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) was already in a steep downtrend since its March 2024 high of $227. It had lost about 48% of its value in just one year by March 2025.

April arrived like a sledgehammer. Trump's tariff announcements sent the entire semiconductor sector into chaos. Tech stocks were in panic mode. But the past few weeks have brought a different narrative. Trade tensions have cooled with a 90 day tariff pause on China. Market volatility has somewhat settled.

Although AMD has rebounded from its lows significantly but it is still trading at levels that make no sense when you look at the fundamentals and what they have achieved.

See, when Nvidia (NVDA) became the AI infrastructure darling, the narrative was that Nvidia would eat AMD alive in AI. Game over. I think that AMD realized this early on, so they pivoted hard and started building AI accelerators specifically for inference workloads, a massive and growing market in the AI space that demands different capabilities than training.

The results speak volumes. They are developing their own tech stack to compete directly with CUDA. They are also capturing meaningful share in AI inference precisely where the next wave of growth is coming from.

AMD is fundamentally changing from a traditional CPU/GPU provider into a vertically integrated AI infrastructure platform. And with the emergence of inference as the next major growth driver in AI computing, positions AMD for a significant rebound. I am initiating coverage with a Strong Buy rating and a 12-month price target of $175 to $200. The bottom, I believe, is in.

The ZT Systems Acquisition

In March 2025 AMD completed the acquisition of ZT Systems which signals a strategic shift. Rather than being solely a semiconductor supplier they are evolving into a full stack AI infrastructure solutions provider.

The timing of this acquisition was also strategic too. As the AI market went from proof of concept deployments to production scale infrastructure customers are increasingly seeking integrated solutions rather than individual components they assemble themselves.

Nvidia recognized this years ago with their DGX platform which has allowed them to capture higher margins and create deeper customer relationships than traditional component sales would allow. ZT acquisition would provide AMD with immediate access to rack-scale design expertise without the multi-year development timeline associated with building these capabilities organically.

They have already proven their ability to design and optimize large scale AI infrastructure for the hyperscalers. They are planning to integrate ZT's design teams into their Data Center Solutions business so they will be able to offer AI solutions that compete directly with Nvidia's ecosystem while leveraging superior economics in inference workloads.

From a financial perspective, this acquisition represents a calculated bet on the systems level integration trend in AI infrastructure.

The was acquisition was valued at $4.9 billion but the structure of this deal was that AMD will divest ZT's manufacturing division so they integrated ZT Systems' design teams into the AMD Data Center Solutions business unit.

Rather than simply buying the entire company, AMD plan was to keep only what it truly needed by paying $4.9 billion for ZT Systems but then recouping $3 billion from selling the manufacturing business to Sanmina they effectively paid $1.9 billion for the intellectual capital that mattered most to them.

What AMD really wanted was ZT's 15+ years of experience in designing rack-scale AI infrastructure for hyperscalers. This expertise allows AMD to transform from merely selling chips to offering complete, integrated AI systems that can compete directly with Nvidia's DGX offerings.

ROCm Ecosystem: Breaking Down the CUDA Fortress

I think that AMD's ROCm software platform is the most underappreciated aspect of their competitive positioning. In my opinion ROCm has crossed a threshold from "good enough" to "genuinely competitive" with Nvidia's CUDA ecosystem.

The fact that over 2 million models on Hugging Face now run natively on AMD hardware is a huge expansion of the addressable software ecosystem. More importantly, the day-zero support for major models like Meta's LLama 4, Google's Gemma 3 and DeepSeek's R1 signals that AMD has moved beyond playing catch-up to becoming a first-tier platform for AI development.

The performance optimizations they delivered for DeepSeek's R1 model enabled MI300X to achieve top spot in inferencing throughput within weeks of the model's release. This shows that they can adapt quickly to new model architectures. Which is a crucial capability in an environment where new models with different computational requirements are getting released constantly.

CUDA has a huge developer community due to its first-mover advantage. The recent increase of open source models from tech companies following DeepSeek R1 release, has boosted engagement of AMD's open source ROCm community. As more developers gain experience with ROCm they contribute optimizations and identify performance bottlenecks accelerating the platform's development beyond what AMD could achieve internally.

ROCm's open source nature appeals to big companies concerned about vendor lock-in and proprietary software dependencies. As companies increasingly adopt multi vendor AI strategies to reduce risk and optimize costs, AMD's open ecosystem provides a solid alternative to Nvidia's more closed approach.

The Inference Strategy

The market environment in AI infrastructure is often oversimplified into a binary AMD versus Nvidia comparison, but I believe this framework misses the reality of how different workloads favor different architectures and approaches.

While Nvidia maintains clear advantages in training workloads through their superior raw compute throughput and its CUDA software ecosystem, AMD's position in inference workloads is a different strategy that doesn't require head-to-head competition in Nvidia.

Inference usually involves heavy memory usage. To better understand this let's take a look at AMD's MI300X, which has 192GB of HBM3 memory and it can handle large model inference tasks that would otherwise require multiple Nvidia H100s working together each with only 80GB of memory.

This gives them a technical advantage in inference workload. it translates directly into cost savings for customers deploying large scale inference infrastructure.

On top of their architecture avoids the memory bottlenecks that can force customers to overprovision Nvidia based systems. A typical 70 billion parameter model requires approximately 140GB of memory just for the model weights meaning it cannot fit on a single H100 without model parallelism techniques that add latency. AMD's MI300X handles these models natively with room for batching multiple queries potentially delivering 2-3x better cost efficiency for inference workloads.

Nvidia's advantages in brand recognition, ecosystem maturity and broad hardware portfolio remain substantial for customers prioritizing maximum performance over cost optimization.

But I believe that the inference market is large enough and diverse enough to support multiple strong competitors especially as customer requirements become more sophisticated and cost consciousness increases.

As we are seeing a new trend of reasoning models that require dozens or hundreds of inference steps per query is reshaping the performance requirements in ways that favor AMD's memory centric architecture. These workloads are less about peak compute throughput and more about efficiently managing large amounts of model state and intermediate results precisely the applications where their design excels.

Can AMD Achieve $175

Before we look at the valuation I want to give you guys a context from a technical analysis perspective that how price action is setting up better understand how the future valuation might look like.

From a technical perspective AMD’s price action has recently shifted in a way that strongly suggests a medium to long-term trend reversal is underway.

Monthly Chart

AMD had been stuck in a downtrend since peaking at $227 in March 2024 with a series of lower highs and lower lows throughout the rest of the year. This decline was not just a correction, it was a full blown reset of sentiment, driven by macro volatility including tariff headlines and capital rotation into mega cap AI names like NVIDIA.

However, I want to look at something interesting in the chart above. Look at the April monthly candle.

  • April’s monthly candle formed a bullish engulfing pattern, reclaiming the $100 level with strong volume behind it was an early signal that buyers were stepping back in with conviction.

  • May’s candle confirms the shift. Not only has it broken above the downtrend line, but it also closed above the 50 EMA ( $111) which had acted as resistance for the past two quarters. This monthly breakout is highly significant from a structural standpoint and suggests the beginning of a new uptrend cycle.

Weekly Chart

Zooming into the weekly chart provides more similar confirmation of the bottoming process.

  • AMD broke out of a multi-month falling channel that had kept price suppressed since early Q2 2024. This breakout decisively cleared the 200 EMA, a key long-term trend indicator now acting as support.

  • RSI has broken its downtrend and is pushing upward from oversold territory (30s). The breakout from the RSI downtrend line creates a positive bullish divergence that signals strong momentum.

I know AMD is a heavily covered stock on SA and across the financial media, so I won’t be doing a traditional valuation analysis. Most of you are probably already familiar with the basics. Instead my focus is not on what the company is worth today but what it could become in the near and distant future. With that in mind let’s get into it

Here's where it gets interesting, AMD generated $939 million in operating cash flow and $727 million in free cash flow. That's a free cash flow margin of nearly 10% which might not sound impressive until you realize this represents a 92% increase Y/Y while revenue only grew 36%. Which means that each additional dollar of revenue is falling to the bottom line at an accelerating rate.

This is why I structured my DCF as a three stage model. In Stage 1 from 2025 to 2027, what I call the "AI Acceleration Phase."

Here's my reasoning: AMD's data center business which generated $3.7 billion in Q1 (up 57% YoY), is operating with a 25% operating margin. But more importantly this segment now represents about half of their total business up from roughly 35% just two years ago. When a company's highest margin, fastest growing segment becomes the dominant revenue driver you get exponential effects on overall profitability.

For this phase, I am projecting a 22% revenue CAGR which might seem aggressive until you consider that AMD's data center business alone is already running at a roughly $15 billion annual rate. With MI350 GPUs ramping in mid-2025 and the ZT Systems integration providing rack-scale solutions I actually think this could prove conservative. In this phase free cash flow margin would expand from 10% to 15% due the mix shift toward higher margin data center products.

My Stage 2 framework, covering 2028-2032 assumes what I call the "Platform Maturation Phase." By this point, AMD will have fully integrated ZT Systems and should be competing not just on individual components but on complete AI infrastructure solutions. The 12% revenue CAGR here is for a more mature but still growing market, while the 15-17% free cash flow margins assume they have successfully transitioned from being a component supplier to being a platform provider.

The terminal stage assumptions 3.5% growth and 18% free cash flow margins might actually be where I'm being most conservative. If they successfully establish themselves as the number two player in AI infrastructure behind Nvidia, 18% margins could prove to be on the low side. But I would rather be conservative in my base case and be pleasantly surprised.

When I run these numbers through my DCF model using a 9.5% WACC, I get an intrinsic value of $168 per share. AMD is transitioning from a cyclical semiconductor company to a more stable AI infrastructure provider which should command a lower risk premium over time.

Segment-Level Valuation

The DCF gives me confidence in the overall direction but I wanted to stress test my assumptions by looking at AMD segment by segment.

Let's start with the Data Center segment, which I think deserves the most attention. In Q1 this business generated $3.7 billion in revenue with a 25% operating margin. Annualized, that's nearly $15 billion in revenue with operating income of roughly $3.7 billion.

The operating leverage in this business is extraordinary. When AMD's data center revenue grew 57% YoY, the operating income grew 72%. That tells me the infrastructure they have built can scale efficiently and each additional customer or deployment has outsized profitability impact.

For my 2027 projections, I'm projecting $30 billion in data center revenue. This assumes AMD maintains its current growth trajectory and captures additional market share as MI350 and MI400 products ramp. At an 8x EV/Sales multiple, this segment alone justifies a $240 billion valuation. Now, you might ask why only 8x when Nvidia trades at 16x+ for its business.

My answer: AMD is still building its moat. Nvidia has CUDA ecosystem lock-in that justifies premium multiples. AMD is working toward that with ROCm, but they're not there yet. Still, 8x for a business growing 40%+ annually with 25%+ margins feels conservative.

The Client and Gaming segment tells a different story. This business generated $2.9 billion in Q1 up 28% YoY but more importantly operating margins expanded from 10% to 17%. This tells me that they are focusing on higher value products like Ryzen AI processors.

I'm projecting $12 billion in 2027 revenue for this segment driven primarily by AI PC adoption and market share gains in commercial notebooks. At 4x EV/Sales this gives us $48 billion in value. Some of you might say this seems high for what's essentially a PC business but I think you are missing that when every laptop needs neural processing units and AMD has competitive offerings at multiple price points the addressable market expands significantly.

The Embedded segment provides valuable diversification and cash generation. At $4 billion in 2027 revenue and 3x EV/Sales it contributes $12 billion to the value. This might seem modest, but embedded tends to be a stickier, higher margin business with longer customer relationships.

Adding it all up: $240 billion + $48 billion + $12 billion = $300 billion in enterprise value. Add back AMD's net cash position of approximately $3.1 billion, and you get $303.1 billion. Divide by 1.64 billion shares outstanding and you arrive at $185 per share.

I believe strongly in the $175 price target but I am not naive about the risks.

In my bear case, where export controls remain in place and competition intensifies, I still see $140 as a floor. Here's why: even in this scenario, AMD's data center CPU business continues gaining market share, the client business benefits from AI PC adoption and they maintain their technological edge in specific AI workloads. At $140 AMD would trade at roughly 20x forward earnings, which seems reasonable for a company still growing revenues at 15%+ annually.

The base case of $175 assumes export controls are partially resolved, MI350 ramp proceeds as planned and AMD achieves 15% market share in AI inference workloads. This scenario feels most probable based on current trends and management guidance.

The bull case pushes above $200 in case of full resolution of export controls, accelerated AI market growth and successful execution on rack-scale solutions. While this scenario depends on several variables going AMD's way simultaneously it's not outside the realm of possibility given the momentum I see in the business.

Your Takeaway

At current prices around $115, AMD offers upside to multiple scenarios with downside protection from its diversified business model. That's the kind of asymmetric risk/reward profile that makes for compelling long-term investments. The market will eventually recognize what AMD has quietly built while everyone was watching the GPU wars.

When it does, $175 won't seem like a target, it'll seem like just the beginning. The rebound may already be beginning - the question is whether investors will recognize it before it becomes obvious.

data.title: ATRenew Q1 Earnings: Strong Fundamentals And Potential Future Upside
data.description:
ATRenew (NYSE:RERE) has recently reported its quarterly earnings results. As always, the Chinese recycling company has recorded substantial sales growth and improving profitability figures.

My previous coverage of ATRenew

In my previous article about ATRenew, I covered the full 2024 annual results. ATRenew reported strong sales growth and positive GAAP net income. The recycling company’s annual 2024 sales increased by 25.9% year-on-year. In my previous work on ATRenew, I also mentioned that the company's cooperation with JD.com (JD) and government subsidies have significantly supported its business and demand for pre-owned electronics. I also wrote about the company’s solid financial fundamentals, a negative net debt, sound liquidity ratios, and the management’s share buyback program. I also said that ATRenew was on track for full-year GAAP profitability in 2025.

Now that ATRenew has just published its 1Q 2025 results, my forecast seems to be accurate. The positive net GAAP profit trend has continued, while the company’s sales have substantially increased compared to the same period a year ago. Let me give you more details about ATRenew’s 1Q results.

Results summary and the company’s news

Total net revenues increased by 27.5% and totaled RMB 4,653.5 million, or $641.3 million, compared to RMB 3,651.1 million for the same period a year ago. The GAAP net profit totaled RMB 42.8 million compared to a net loss of RMB 92.9 million in the same period of 2024. The non-GAAP net income totaled RMB 78 million, or $10.8 million, a rise of 276.8% compared to RMB 20.7 million for the same period a year ago. The number of consumer products transacted totaled 9.5 million compared to 8.4 million in the same period of 2024.

So, we can easily see that ATRenew’s fundamentals are quickly improving.

The company’s management says the company’s brilliant results were due to the national subsidy policies, ATRenew’s improved fulfillment capabilities, and substantial retail network.

In the first quarter, the company’s total sales increased due to sustained growth in the company’s net product revenues. These rose by 28.8% to RMB 4,263.7 million thanks to a rise in the sales of pre-owned consumer electronics via the company’s online channels. Net service sales totaled RMB 389.8 million, an increase of 14.2%. The rise was primarily due to the service revenue surge resulting from the multi-category recycling business and PJT Marketplace. The company’s marketplaces' overall gross transaction value increased in the first quarter of 2025. The multi-category recycling businesses contributed more than RMB 50 million, or 13.3% of service revenues, in the first quarter, a substantial increase from 5.6% in the same period a year ago.

In the first quarter, ATRenew’s innovative AI applications, from Smart Pricing to the integrated Smart Supply Chain System, have significantly boosted operational efficiency and driven industry transformation, advancing the standardized evolution of the “AI + Circular Economy” model. ATRenew is driving the commercialization of AI in the second-hand electronics industry through the deployment of open-source large language models (LLMs) and proprietary systems such as the 007 Functional Inspection Module, X-ray Module, Camera Box System, Camera Magic Mirror Module, and Godzilla Module. Powered by data from processing over 90,000 devices daily, ATRenew has significantly improved inspection efficiency and accuracy. Compared to traditional manual methods, its Matrix Automated Quality Inspection System cuts labor costs by nearly 80% and increases automation efficiency by over 90%, with precision reaching 0.01% and accuracy up to 99%.

Also, ATRenew’s strong cash flow supports growth and maximizes shareholder returns. In uncertain times, strong cash flow serves as a solid foundation for both growth and shareholder returns. ATRenew ended Q1 2025 with RMB 2.78 billion in cash, restricted cash, short-term investments, and third-party receivables—reflecting strong cash flow and financial flexibility. Backed by these cash reserves, the company was able to buy back 0.4 million ADSs for $1.2 million during the quarter under its $50 million buyback program. As of March 31, total repurchases reached 10.7 million ADSs, worth $27.1 million, underscoring its commitment to shareholder returns.

But how do the recent results compare to the previous quarterly results?

ATRenew’s quarterly earnings history

Here is ATRenew’s quarterly earnings history.

The numbers are rounded to the nearest million RMB.

As can be seen from the table above and the graph below, the sales have been rising sustainably. The GAAP net profit has been positive for the third consecutive quarter.

Also, the non-GAAP net profit has been rising for a while. The non-GAAP net profit has risen substantially since 1Q 2024.

So, in the context of the recent history, it seems that ATRenew’s earnings and sales are improving on all fronts. Moreover, the company’s stock valuations are low despite the recent positive results.

The company’s valuations

The diagram above shows ATRenew’s stock price since the company’s IPO in 2021. It is trading quite low right now, but its stock used to trade over and above $15 per share. Its fundamentals, as I have mentioned above, have been sustainably improving. So, RERE stock seems to be really cheap right now.

Moreover, as can be seen from the ratio analysis conducted by Seeking Alpha, most of the valuation ratios related to the company’s performance suggest strong undervaluation. Only the GAAP price-to-earnings ratio suggests overvaluation. However, this ratio will likely improve very soon. As I have mentioned above, the company’s net GAAP earnings are rising, and the company is on track to become net GAAP positive for the full 2025.

Upside factors

Among the upside factors are the Chinese government’s strong support for the recycling industry and also the general public in China getting more environmentally conscious.

During the 5th China International Consumer Products Expo (CICPE), China announced a list of pilot cities and companies for secondhand goods circulation. ATRenew, a leader in China's circular economy, was named a national pilot enterprise in this initiative.

The program, launched by China, takes a "localized and category-focused" approach. Over the next three years, it will particularly support sectors like secondhand smartphones, clothing, and furniture. The initiative’s purpose is to establish replicable models driving high-quality development in the industry. Companies like ATRenew are expected to spearhead green consumption practices and promote low-carbon lifestyles in commercial sectors.

ATRenew has built a robust supply chain to enable efficient circulation of pre-owned goods, raising the national trade-in campaign. The enterprise has mastered core competencies in secondhand supply chains, creating a closed-loop ecosystem that integrates "collection, processing, and resale."

The growing interest of consumers, China’s government, and investors is highly positive for ATRenew’s stock price. As I have mentioned above, ATRenew has been reporting sustainable sales growth. It is set to become net GAAP-profitable in 2025. The stock buyback program has also been ongoing for a while, which can add more positive upside pressure for the company’s stock. Its valuations, however, are very moderate. In other words, it is an undervalued growth stock. So, now it seems to be a good opportunity to invest in ATRenew.

Downside risks

Among the downside risks, however, are the trade relations between the US and China. These could have a negative effect on investors’ sentiments. At the same time, ATRenew is a Chinese company that does not engage in international trade, and American consumers are not its clients. So, the business itself will be unaffected, while the stock valuations could be affected in the short term. Additionally, China’s rapid rise in AI and technology is expected to lead to a re-rating of Chinese equities despite the trade tensions. ATRenew is well-positioned to benefit from this trend as it keeps advancing its “AI + Circular Economy model. I believe ATRenew has tremendous business potential in leveraging AI technology, particularly in driving AI commercialization in the second-hand electronics industry.

There is also a general recession risk for the global economy that is highly likely to make most asset classes, including ATRenew’s stock, depreciate.

Also, some conservative American investors might prefer to invest in US-based companies that pay dividends. However, growth investors are likely to find ATRenew a sound, undervalued, high-growth stock to invest in.

Conclusion

ATRenew has again reported strong earnings, improving sales, and a third consecutive quarter of net GAAP profit. The management’s outlook is positive; the company continues its buyback program, while RERE stock valuations are very low. So, RERE is an asset worth considering for growth-oriented investors interested in sustainable Chinese companies.

data.title: IDLV: Low Volatility Has Translated Into Lower Long-Term Returns
data.description:
On two occasions this year, I have written about the current advantages of investing overseas. In January, I wrote about the Schwab International Equity ETF (SCHF), giving it a buy rating. The fund is up almost 16% year-to-date. Earlier this month, I also reviewed the iShares International Equity Factor ETF (INTF), which has gained just over 17% this year. There is no doubt that overseas equities have performed well in recent months, and some have hypothesized that this recent momentum is just the start of a multi-year trend where international stocks provide better returns than the overall market in the US.

A recent report from Vanguard projected that overseas equities in developed countries could outperform their counterparts in the US by 2.7% over the next ten years. While an extended period of outperformance for international stocks may seem unlikely to many US investors, the past has shown us that superior performance appears to come in waves. Over the last 50 years, when looking at five-year rolling returns, US stocks have topped international stocks for an average of just over eight years in any given cycle. We are currently in a period of outperformance has almost doubled that time frame, as shown in the graph below.

All that being said, not every international stock fund is worth buying. While my reviews of SCHF and INTF were favorable, I do not think the same can be said about the Invesco S&P International Developed Low Volatility ETF (NYSEARCA: IDLV). IDLV has also strung together impressive gains in 2025. I discovered this fund when I saw that it has actually outperformed the other two that I have previously reviewed in the first five months of this year, with its gains approaching 18%.

All three of these international funds are considered members of a broad category of international large-cap blend funds, but their methodology for choosing stocks is quite different. After looking closer into IDLV's stock selection methods and considering its unique country allocation, I think the best days of the year may be behind this ETF, as I do not see it outperforming over a longer period of time.

ETF Description

The Invesco S&P International Developed Low Volatility ETF is designed to follow the performance of the S&P BMI International Developed Low Volatility Index™. This index is designed to include the 200 large and mid-cap stocks in the S&P Developed Broad Market Index that have shown the lowest volatility over the last 12 months. These 200 stocks are weighted so that the least volatile stocks have the highest allocations in the index. The fund is rebalanced and reconstituted on a quarterly basis.

IDLV has a total of $391 million in assets under management. A copy of the fund’s top 10 holdings is given below. As can be seen, this fund is well diversified with no single holding reaching more than 0.70% of the total portfolio. Koninklijke KPN, a Dutch provider of medical systems and consumer health products, currently holds that top spot in the portfolio. The smallest equity holding in the fund belongs to Mapletree Logistics Trust, a REIT based in Singapore, that represents 0.37% of the fund.

Large-cap companies make up about 71% of the fund's portfolio, with mid-caps taking up the balance. While that is similar to the mix found in both INTF and SCHF, this fund has some noticeable differences from a sector weighting perspective. IDLV matches most other international large-cap blend funds with its largest allocations dedicated to financials and industrials. However, IDLV has a rather sizeable investment in real estate, significantly above the average fund of its type, and the fund also has almost no exposure to technology. Its lone holding in the tech sector is CGI, a Canadian software and IT consulting company.

The Invesco S&P International Developed Low Volatility ETF also deviates from the other funds mentioned here with its unique country allocation. Over one-fifth of the fund is invested in Canadian stocks. Another 10% is dedicated to companies in Australia. This is significantly more than the typical overseas large-cap blend fund and well ahead of INTF's or SCHF's allocation for those two countries. The company is underweight in Japan.

IDLV charges a management fee of 0.25%, which is slightly higher than the typical passively managed fund but lower than most international funds. The fund has a dividend yield of 2.97%. Dividends from IDLV have actually been on a downward trend over the last couple of years. 2024 marked the third straight year of decreasing dividends for the fund.

Fund Analysis

While IDLV will continue to benefit from the overall gains made in international markets, there are a few reasons why I am avoiding recommending the ETF as a buy at the current time.

The first is the fund’s relative underperformance when compared to other developed markets international funds over the long term. Consider the returns for IDLV when compared to INTF and SCHF over the last 10 years. IDLV has certainly impressed over the last year, but the difference in returns over the three-, five-, and ten-year periods is striking. If dividends are not included in total returns, IDLV has barely broken even over the last decade.

My second concern for this ETF is the fund’s country allocations with heavy weights in Canada and Australia. The economy in Canada is under pressure from a number of factors. The first is the potential for continued trade conflict with the US. The implementation of new tariffs by the US, even if less than previously announced in early April, could cause disruptions in growth projections which are already rather modest. S&P Global has projected that the country could be teetering on the edge of a recession later this year. Oxford Economics has projected that Canada will have a prolonged recession that starts during the current quarter and does not end until sometime in 2026, as shown in the chart below.

The unemployment rate in Canada is expected to be between 6.6% and 7% through the remainder of 2025. This number could be even higher if trade wars cause a more significant downturn in economic activity.

Australia is also projected to have higher unemployment in the next 12 months. Wage growth in that country is expected to decline after a strong showing in 2024. Business investment is projected to be weak until the final months of this year. The Australian stock market has been trading at above average valuations this year, buoyed in part by declining interest rates.

The final point of caution that I have for IDLV is that it seems to hold a portfolio of stocks that have a slightly higher valuation than the other two funds mentioned in this analysis. The stocks contained in SCHF do have a higher PE ratio, but IDLV has the highest Price to Book and Price to Cash flow ratios of the trio. It also has declining dividend payouts and the lowest long-term earnings growth.
